what: Create Box or Violin Plots with Statistical Comparisons — plot_diff
title: Create Box or Violin Plots with Statistical Comparisons — plot_diff
text: function generates box plots violin plots statistical tests pairwise comparisons. automatically selects appropriate statistical tests based assumptions, allows manual specification. Statistical testing performed using companion function run_diff.

title: Create Box or Violin Plots with Statistical Comparisons — plot_diff
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Example 1: Show mean dot AND mean value results_anova <- plot_diff(   x            = PlantGrowth,   outcome      = "weight",   group        = "group",   plot_type    = "boxplot",   show_mean    = TRUE,   show_agg_val = TRUE ) print(results_anova$plots$weight)  # Example 2: Global font size controls all text proportionally results_anova2 <- plot_diff(   x               = PlantGrowth,   outcome         = "weight",   group           = "group",   global_font_size = 16,   show_global_p   = TRUE  # show the Kruskal-Wallis / ANOVA p-value on the plot ) print(results_anova2$plots$weight)  # Example 3: Paired t-test with custom subtitle, formatted p-values sleep_data     <- sleep sleep_data$ID  <- rep(1:10, 2) results_paired <- plot_diff(   x              = sleep_data,   outcome        = "extra",   group          = "group",   paired         = TRUE,   plot_subtitle  = "Paired comparison: Drug 1 vs Drug 2",   show_p_numeric = TRUE,   format_p_numeric = 4  # 4 decimal places ) print(results_paired$plots$extra) } # }

title: Plot PCA Scores Plot — plot_score

title: Plot PCA Scores Plot — plot_score
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data set.seed(123) x <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 50, mean = 100, sd = 20),             nrow = 100, ncol = 50) colnames(x) <- paste0("Feature", 1:50) rownames(x) <- paste0("Sample", 1:100)  metadata <- data.frame(   Sample = paste0("Sample", 1:100),   Group = rep(c("Control", "Treatment", "QC"), c(40, 40, 20)),   Batch = rep(1:4, each = 25),   Time = rep(c(0, 6, 12, 24), 25) )  # Run PCA (excluding QC samples) scaled_result <- run_scale(x, method = "auto") pca_result <- run_pca(scaled_result$data, metadata,                        group = "Group",                       exclude = "QC")  # Basic scores plot (colored by Group, labeled by Sample) plot_score(pca_result,             color_by = "Group",            points_from = "Sample")  # PC2 vs PC3 with custom title plot_score(pca_result,             pc = c(2, 3),            color_by = "Group",            points_from = "Sample",            title = "PCA: PC2 vs PC3",            position = "left")  # Color by batch plot_score(pca_result,            color_by = "Batch",            points_from = "Sample")  # Continuous coloring by time with custom legend plot_score(pca_result,            color_by = "Time",            points_from = "Sample",            legend = "Collection Time (h)")  # Show outliers in specific groups plot_score(pca_result,            color_by = "Group",            points_from = "Sample",            show_outliers = TRUE,            label_outliers = c("Control", "Treatment"),            ellipse_level = 0.95)  # Stricter outlier detection (99% confidence) plot_score(pca_result,            color_by = "Group",            points_from = "Sample",            show_outliers = TRUE,            ellipse_level = 0.99) } # }

title: Plot PCA Scree Plot — plot_scree
text: Scree Plot Interpretation: scree plot helps determine many principal components retain: Elbow Method: Look "elbow" curve flattens. PCs elbow capture meaningful variance; capture mostly noise. Kaiser Criterion: Retain PCs eigenvalues > 1 (= "eigen"). PCs explain variance single original variable. Cumulative Variance: Retain enough PCs explain 70-90% total variance (shown show_cumulative = TRUE). Plot Types: bar: Traditional bar chart, good discrete comparison line: Line plot, better seeing trends : Combines bars line comprehensive view Cumulative Variance Line: show_cumulative = TRUE, line showing cumulative variance added plot secondary y-axis top. helps visualize many PCs needed explain target percentage variance (e.g., 80%). Use Metric: Variance explained: intuitive (percentages). Recommended users. Eigenvalues: Useful Kaiser criterion advanced analysis.

title: Volcano Plot for Fold Change and P-value Data — plot_volcano
code: ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 1 — minimal synthetic data, no group ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(42) n   <- 200 dat <- data.frame(   feature = paste0("feat_", seq_len(n)),   log2fc  = rnorm(n, 0, 2),   pvalue  = runif(n, 0, 0.5) )  res <- plot_volcano(x = dat, y = "log2fc", z = "pvalue") print(res$plots[[1]])   ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 2 — asymmetric thresholds using log2() directly, with labels ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ann <- setNames(c("feat_1", "feat_2", "feat_3"),                 c("Marker A", "Marker B", "Marker C"))  res2 <- plot_volcano(   x        = dat,   y        = "log2fc",   z        = "pvalue",   features = "feature",   up       = log2(1.5),   down     = log2(0.5),   annotate = ann ) print(res2$plots[[1]])   ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 3 — multi-group overlay ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(7) dat_multi <- data.frame(   feature    = rep(paste0("feat_", seq_len(50)), 3),   log2fc     = rnorm(150, 0, 1.5),   pvalue     = runif(150, 0, 0.3),   comparison = rep(c("Severe_vs_Healthy", "Nonsevere_vs_Healthy",                      "Severe_vs_Nonsevere"), each = 50) )  res3 <- plot_volcano(   x           = dat_multi,   y           = "log2fc",   z           = "pvalue",   group       = "comparison",   features    = "feature",   annotate    = setNames(c("feat_1", "feat_5"), c("Marker A", "Marker B")),   alpha       = 0.5,   legend_nrow = 2 ) print(res3$plots[[1]])

what: Compute WHO Anthropometric Z-Scores and Nutritional Status — run_anthroindex
title: Compute WHO Anthropometric Z-Scores and Nutritional Status — run_anthroindex
text: Computes Child Growth Standards z-scores eight anthropometric indices—weight--age (WFA), length/height--age (HFA), weight--length/height (WFH), BMI--age (BMIFA), head circumference--age (HCFA), arm circumference--age (ACFA), triceps skinfold--age (TSFA), subscapular skinfold--age (SSFA)—using anthro_zscores (Child Growth Standards 2006) ages 0 < 5 years, anthroplus_zscores (Growth Reference 2007) ages 5 < 20 years applicable. BMIFA, function covers full age range: 0 < 5 years: z-scores anthro_zscores, Child Growth Standards 2006. 5 < 20 years: z-scores anthroplus_zscores, Growth Reference 2007. anthroplus package must installed separately participants age range present. 20 years : nutritional status classified directly computed BMI value using adult cut-offs (z-score). Nutritional Status column appended result according classification thresholds.

title: Compute WHO Anthropometric Z-Scores and Nutritional Status — run_anthroindex
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(pondeR)  if (requireNamespace("anthro", quietly = TRUE)) {    # --- Synthetic dataset (no external data file required) ----------------   set.seed(42)   n <- 50   children <- data.frame(     child_id = seq_len(n),     sex      = sample(c("Male", "Female"), n, replace = TRUE),     age_mo   = runif(n, 1, 60),     wt_kg    = runif(n, 3, 20),     ht_cm    = runif(n, 50, 115),     oedema   = sample(c("n", "y"), n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.95, 0.05))   )    # --- Multiple indices — returns a named list ---------------------------   res <- run_anthroindex(     x               = children,     sex_col         = "sex",     age_col         = "age_mo",     age_unit        = "month",     weight_col      = "wt_kg",     weight_unit     = "kg",     height_col      = "ht_cm",     height_unit     = "cm",     height_measured = "L",     oedema_col      = "oedema",     return          = c("wfa", "hfa", "wfh", "bmifa")   )    head(res$wfa[,  c("z_score", "Nutritional Status")])   head(res$bmifa[, c("cbmi", "z_score", "Nutritional Status")])    # --- Single index — returns a plain data frame -------------------------   wfa_only <- run_anthroindex(     x               = children,     sex_col         = "sex",     age_col         = "age_mo",     age_unit        = "month",     weight_col      = "wt_kg",     weight_unit     = "kg",     height_col      = "ht_cm",     height_unit     = "cm",     height_measured = "L",     return          = "wfa"   )   head(wfa_only)    # --- Mixed-age dataset spanning all three age brackets ----------------   if (requireNamespace("anthroplus", quietly = TRUE)) {     mixed <- data.frame(       sex    = c("Male", "Female", "Male"),       age_yr = c(3, 14, 25),       wt_kg  = c(14, 38.8, 80),       ht_cm  = c(95, 151.13, 175)     )     bmi_res <- run_anthroindex(       x               = mixed,       sex_col         = "sex",       age_col         = "age_yr",       age_unit        = "year",       weight_col      = "wt_kg",       height_col      = "ht_cm",       height_measured = "H",       return          = "bmifa"     )     bmi_res[, c("age_yr", "cbmi", "z_score", "Nutritional Status")]   } } } # }

what: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
text: function performs automatic comparison two categorical variables. intelligently selects appropriate statistical test (Chi-square, Fisher's Exact Test, McNemar's Test), calculates measures association, performs post-hoc tests appropriate.

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
code: run_assoc(   x,   var1,   var2,   weight = NULL,   paired = FALSE,   test_type = c("auto", "chisq", "fisher"),   expected_freq_threshold = 5,   continuity_correction = TRUE,   fisher_simulate = TRUE,   test_alpha = 0.05,   calculate_association = TRUE,   perform_posthoc = TRUE,   p_adjust_method = "BH",   verbose = TRUE )

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
text: x data frame containing variables analysis. var1 character string specifying name first categorical variable (typically rows). var2 character string specifying name second categorical variable (typically columns). weight optional character string specifying name weight column. Use x "long" format (e.g., .data.frame.table()). paired logical indicating whether observations paired (McNemar's test). Default FALSE. test_type character string specifying test strategy independent samples. Must one auto (default), chisq, fisher. parameter ignored paired = TRUE. expected_freq_threshold minimum expected cell frequency justify using Chi-square test test_type = "auto". Default 5. continuity_correction logical indicating whether apply Yates' continuity correction 2x2 Chi-square tests. Default TRUE. fisher_simulate logical indicating whether use Monte Carlo simulation Fisher's test p-value tables larger 2x2 (can computationally intensive). Default TRUE. test_alpha Significance level final statistical test. Default 0.05. calculate_association Logical indicating whether calculate measures association (e.g., Cramer's V, Odds Ratio). Default TRUE. perform_posthoc Logical indicating whether perform post-hoc tests significant RxC tables. Default TRUE. p_adjust_method P-value adjustment method post-hoc comparisons. See stats::p.adjust.methods. Default "BH". verbose logical indicating whether print detailed messages. Default TRUE.

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
text: object class "run_assoc" list containing: test_used Character string statistical test performed. test_result List containing results statistical test ('htest' object). contingency_table observed contingency table (data.frame). expected_table expected frequencies table (data.frame, NULL Fisher's/McNemar's). residuals Pearson residuals (data.frame, NULL Fisher's/McNemar's). stdres Standardized residuals (data.frame, NULL Fisher's/McNemar's). association Data frame containing association metrics, magnitude, interpretation. posthoc_result Data frame post-hoc pairwise comparisons (NULL applicable). assumptions Data frame summarizing assumption check results. var1 Name row variable. var2 Name column variable. paired paired setting used. test_alpha significance level used. warnings Character vector warnings generated. parameters List parameters used analysis.

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
text: function follows decision-making process determine appropriate test: Test Family Selection: paired = TRUE, function assumes data matched pairs requires 2x2 table. performs McNemar's Test. paired = FALSE, function assumes independent samples builds contingency table. Test Selection Logic (test_type = "auto" independent samples): choice Chi-square Fisher's test based Cochran's rule. Assumption Check: function first runs Chi-square test calculate expected frequencies cell contingency table. Decision: cell expected frequency less expected_freq_threshold (default 5), Chi-square approximation may inaccurate. function discards result performs Fisher's Exact Test. cells expected frequency >= expected_freq_threshold, function proceeds Pearson's Chi-square Test. Post-Hoc Tests: significant result independent table larger 2x2, post-hoc test performed using rstatix::pairwise_fisher_test determine specific pairs categories driving association. P-values adjusted using method specified p_adjust_method. Measures Association (Effect Sizes): function automatically calculates appropriate measures association: Phi (\(\phi\)) Odds Ratio 2x2 independent tables. Cramer's V RxC independent tables (larger 2x2). Odds Ratio (paired) 2x2 paired (McNemar's) tables.

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # --- Example 1: 2x2 Independent Test (Auto -> Chi-square) --- # Using 'mtcars' to compare transmission (am) vs. engine shape (vs) # This will use Chi-square as all expected counts are > 5. data(mtcars) res_mtcars <- run_assoc(x = mtcars, var1 = "am", var2 = "vs") print(res_mtcars) summary(res_mtcars)  # --- Example 2: RxC Independent Test (Auto -> Chi-square) --- # Using 'HairEyeColor' data, which is a table. # We must first convert it to a data frame and use the 'Freq' column as a weight. data(HairEyeColor) he_data <- as.data.frame.table(HairEyeColor) # We'll just compare Hair vs. Eye he_data_sub <- he_data[he_data$Sex == "Male", ] # Subset to avoid 3D  res_hair <- run_assoc(   x = he_data_sub,   var1 = "Hair",   var2 = "Eye",   weight = "Freq" ) print(res_hair) summary(res_hair) # Will include post-hoc results  # --- Example 3: Paired 2x2 Test (McNemar's) --- # We must create a dataset for this. # Imagine a campaign to get people to vote "Yes" (1). set.seed(42) N <- 100 before_vote <- sample(c("No", "Yes"), N, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.7, 0.3)) after_vote <- before_vote # 20% of "No" voters switch to "Yes" after_vote[before_vote == "No"][1:14] <- "Yes" # 10% of "Yes" voters switch to "No" after_vote[before_vote == "Yes"][1:3] <- "No"  paired_data <- data.frame(   person_id = 1:N,   before = before_vote,   after = after_vote )  res_paired <- run_assoc(   x = paired_data,   var1 = "before",   var2 = "after",   paired = TRUE ) summary(res_paired)  # --- Example 4: Force a test_type --- # Force Chi-square on the mtcars data, with continuity correction res_mtcars_chisq <- run_assoc(   x = mtcars,   var1 = "am",   var2 = "vs",   test_type = "chisq",   continuity_correction = TRUE ) print(res_mtcars_chisq) } # }

title: Comprehensive Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC) Analysis — run_auc
code: ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 1 — numeric binary response (0/1), all predictors ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(42) n  <- 120 df <- data.frame(   group    = sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE),   marker_A = rnorm(n, mean = ifelse(sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE) == 1, 1, 0)),   marker_B = rnorm(n),   marker_C = runif(n) )  res <- run_auc(x = df, y = "group", plot = "none") print(res$auc_table) #>   predictor       auc  ci_lower  ci_upper ci_level direction n_cases n_controls #> 1  marker_A 0.5133765 0.4085016 0.6182514     0.95         >      67         53 #> 2  marker_B 0.5243593 0.4185301 0.6301886     0.95         <      67         53 #> 3  marker_C 0.5350605 0.4300641 0.6400569     0.95         >      67         53  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 2 — categorical response, remove one group, combined plot ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- df2 <- data.frame(   status   = sample(c("control", "case", "unknown"), n, replace = TRUE),   score_1  = rnorm(n),   score_2  = rnorm(n) )  res2 <- run_auc(   x           = df2,   y           = "status",   remove      = "unknown",   set_control = "control",   plot        = "none",   compute_ci  = FALSE ) print(res2$auc_table) #>   predictor       auc ci_lower ci_upper ci_level direction n_cases n_controls #> 1   score_1 0.5257453       NA       NA     0.95         >      36         41 #> 2   score_2 0.5372629       NA       NA     0.95         <      36         41  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 3 — column names with spaces, select top-2 by AUC ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- df3 <- data.frame(   `my group`  = sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE),   `feature 1` = rnorm(n, mean = 0.5),   `feature 2` = rnorm(n),   check.names = FALSE )  res3 <- run_auc(   x    = df3,   y    = "my group",   plot = "none" ) print(res3$auc_table) #>   predictor       auc  ci_lower  ci_upper ci_level direction n_cases n_controls #> 1 feature 1 0.5167558 0.4120382 0.6214735     0.95         >      67         53 #> 2 feature 2 0.5516756 0.4461648 0.6571863     0.95         >      67         53

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison with Comprehensive Analysis — run_diff
text: function follows decision-making process determine appropriate test: Test Family Selection: paired = FALSE, compares independent groups. 2 groups, selects two-sample test family. 2 groups, selects ANOVA-family test. Assumption Checking (test_type = "auto"): Normality: Assessed using Shapiro-Wilk test small samples (n < 50). larger samples (n >= 50), uses Lilliefors (Kolmogorov-Smirnov) test nortest package. two groups, group tested. two groups, normality model residuals tested. Homogeneity Variance: independent tests, assessed using Levene's test car package. car installed, falls back Bartlett's test. Test Selection Logic (test_type = "auto"): Independent Two-Sample: normality violated -> Mann-Whitney U test (Wilcoxon rank-sum). normality OK variance violated -> Welch's t-test. OK -> Student's t-test. Paired Two-Sample: normality violated -> Wilcoxon signed-rank test. normality OK -> Paired t-test. Independent ANOVA (>2 groups): normality violated -> Kruskal-Wallis test. normality OK variance violated -> Welch's ANOVA. OK -> One-way ANOVA. Post-Hoc Tests: significant results >2 groups, appropriate post-hoc tests automatically performed: Tukey HSD one-way ANOVA Games-Howell Welch's ANOVA Dunn's test Kruskal-Wallis Effect Sizes: function automatically calculates appropriate effect sizes: Cohen's d t-tests Rank-biserial correlation Mann-Whitney U Eta-squared ANOVA Epsilon-squared Kruskal-Wallis

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison with Comprehensive Analysis — run_diff
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # --- Basic Usage with 3+ Groups --- # Using the classic 'iris' dataset. Sepal.Length is numeric, Species is a factor. # This will likely trigger the One-way ANOVA -> Tukey HSD pathway. data(iris) res_iris <- run_diff(x = iris, outcome = "Sepal.Length", group = "Species") print(res_iris) summary(res_iris)  # --- Two-Sample Independent Comparison --- # Using 'mtcars' to compare miles per gallon (mpg) between automatic (0) and manual (1) cars. # The variances are unequal, so this will trigger the Welch's t-test. data(mtcars) mtcars$am <- factor(mtcars$am, labels = c("automatic", "manual")) res_mtcars <- run_diff(x = mtcars, outcome = "mpg", group = "am") print(res_mtcars)  # --- Paired-Sample Comparison --- # Using the 'sleep' dataset, which is designed for a paired t-test. # It compares the extra hours of sleep for the same subjects under two drugs. data(sleep) # We specify the subject ID is implicit in the row order for a paired test. res_sleep <- run_diff(x = sleep, outcome = "extra", group = "group", paired = TRUE) summary(res_sleep)  # --- Forcing a Non-Parametric Test --- # Use 'iris' data again, but force the non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis test. data(iris) res_iris_np <- run_diff(   x = iris,   outcome = "Sepal.Length",   group = "Species",   test_type = "nonparametric" ) print(res_iris_np)  # --- Forcing a Parametric Test & Custom P-Value Adjustment --- # Use the 'ToothGrowth' data with 'dose' as the grouping factor. # We force an ANOVA (even if normality is slightly off) and use Bonferroni correction for post-hocs. data(ToothGrowth) # Convert dose to a factor to be treated as a group ToothGrowth$dose <- as.factor(ToothGrowth$dose) res_tooth <- run_diff(   x = ToothGrowth,   outcome = "len",   group = "dose",   test_type = "parametric",   p_adjust_method = "bonferroni" ) # Look at the post-hoc results and the adjusted p-values print(res_tooth$posthoc_result)  # --- Demonstrating Custom Group Ordering --- # Use 'PlantGrowth' and specify a non-alphabetical order for the groups. data(PlantGrowth) custom_order <- c("trt1", "ctrl", "trt2") res_plant <- run_diff(   x = PlantGrowth,   outcome = "weight",   group = "group",   group_order = custom_order,   verbose = FALSE # Suppress messages for cleaner output ) # The summary and plots will respect the custom order print(res_plant$data_summary)  # --- Disabling Features for Quicker Analysis --- # If you only need the main test result, you can disable effect sizes and post-hoc tests. # Using 'mtcars' again with the 'cyl' variable as a group. data(mtcars) mtcars$cyl <- as.factor(mtcars$cyl) res_mtcars_fast <- run_diff(   x = mtcars,   outcome = "mpg",   group = "cyl",   calculate_effect_size = FALSE,   perform_posthoc = FALSE ) print(res_mtcars_fast) } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data with drift and batch effects set.seed(123) n_samples <- 120 n_features <- 50 x <- matrix(rnorm(n_samples * n_features, mean = 100, sd = 20),             nrow = n_samples, ncol = n_features) colnames(x) <- paste0("Feature", 1:n_features)  # Add systematic drift drift <- seq(0, 30, length.out = n_samples) x <- x + drift  metadata <- data.frame(   Sample = paste0("S", 1:n_samples),   InjectionSequence = 1:n_samples,   Batches = rep(1:3, each = 40),   Group = rep(c(rep("Sample", 9), "QC"), n_samples / 10) )  # Apply QCRSC correction result1 <- run_driftBatchCorrect(x, metadata)  # Apply ComBat correction only result2 <- run_driftBatchCorrect(x, metadata, batch_corr_only = TRUE) } # }

title: Filter Features by Relative Standard Deviation in QC Samples — run_filterRSD
text: Relative Standard Deviation (RSD): RSD calculated : RSD = (SD / Mean) × 100% Also known coefficient variation (CV), RSD expresses variability percentage mean, making scale-independent suitable comparing features different magnitudes. Filter RSD: Quality control samples low variability since represent biological matrix. High RSD QC samples indicates: Poor analytical reproducibility Instrument instability Ion suppression/enhancement effects Features near detection limit Features high QC RSD unreliable removed biological interpretation. Choosing QC Type: EQC (default): commonly used. Represents diluted pooled samples analyzed regularly throughout run. SQC: Undiluted pooled samples. Use specific SQC type distinct EQC. QC: Use distinguish QC types, want assess RSD across QC samples combined. Typical RSD Thresholds: Stringent: 20% (0.2) - high-quality data Standard: 30% (0.3) - recommended metabolomics studies Lenient: 40% (0.4) - sample size limited

title: Filter Features by Missing Value Threshold — run_filtermissing
text: function filters features based proportion missing values (NAs). Zero values treated missing metabolomics data, zero values often represent one following: Peak intensities detection limit Failed peak integration Metabolites detected sample zero_as_missing = TRUE (default), zero values converted missing values filtering. recommended untargeted metabolomics datasets. Set zero_as_missing = FALSE one following true: preprocessing pipeline already converted zeros NA Zero represents true biological absence quantitative meaning dataset contains negative values (example baseline correction) Filtering strategies Two filtering modes available: Global filtering (filter_by_group = FALSE): Missingness calculated across eligible samples. Features missing proportion greater equal threshold removed. Group-wise filtering (filter_by_group = TRUE): Missingness calculated separately within group. feature retained satisfies threshold least one group. approach less stringent helps preserve group-specific features. code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data with varying levels of variance set.seed(123) n_samples <- 100 n_features <- 50  # Create features with different variance levels x <- matrix(nrow = n_samples, ncol = n_features) for (i in 1:n_features) {   # Variance increases with feature index   sd_val <- i / 2   x[, i] <- rnorm(n_samples, mean = 100, sd = sd_val) } colnames(x) <- paste0("Feature", 1:n_features)  # Remove bottom 10% (5 features with lowest variance) result1 <- run_filtervariance(x, percentile = 10)  # More aggressive filtering (bottom 20%) result2 <- run_filtervariance(x, percentile = 20) } # }

title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
text: Fold change computation Group means computed per feature. \(k\) ordered levels \(g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_k\), fold change comparison \(\) \(\bar{x}_{g_i} / \bar{x}_{g_{+1}}\), yielding \(k - 1\) pairwise comparisons. Log2 shifting log2 = TRUE, let \(m\) global minimum (filtered, selected) data matrix. shift \(\delta = \text{eps} - m\) added every value \(m \le 0\); otherwise shift applied (\(\delta = 0\)). title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
code: ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 1 — basic usage with iris ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- data(iris) x_iris  <- iris[, 1:4] meta_iris <- data.frame(Species = iris$Species)  res <- run_foldchange(   x        = x_iris,   metadata = meta_iris,   group    = "Species" ) print(res$fc_table) #>        feature setosa_vs_versicolor versicolor_vs_virginica #> 1  Sepal.Width            1.2375451               0.9314055 #> 2 Sepal.Length            0.8433288               0.9010322 #> 3 Petal.Length            0.3431925               0.7672911 #> 4  Petal.Width            0.1855204               0.6544916 print(res$log2fc_table) #>        feature setosa_vs_versicolor versicolor_vs_virginica #> 1  Sepal.Width            0.3074811              -0.1025187 #> 2 Sepal.Length           -0.2458328              -0.1503495 #> 3 Petal.Length           -1.5429101              -0.3821541 #> 4  Petal.Width           -2.4303506              -0.6115534  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 2 — custom order, filter one level, select two features ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- res2 <- run_foldchange(   x        = x_iris,   metadata = meta_iris,   group    = "Species",   arrange  = c("virginica", "versicolor", "setosa"),   filter   = NULL,   select   = c("Sepal.Length", "Petal.Length") ) print(res2$comparisons) #> [1] "virginica_vs_versicolor" "versicolor_vs_setosa"    print(res2$fc_table) #>        feature virginica_vs_versicolor versicolor_vs_setosa #> 1 Petal.Length                1.303286             2.913817 #> 2 Sepal.Length                1.109838             1.185777  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 3 — data with special characters in column names ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(1) n   <- 60 xsc <- data.frame(   `LPC 18:2`  = rnorm(n, 5, 1),   `PA O-28:1` = rnorm(n, 3, 0.5),   check.names = FALSE ) meta_sc <- data.frame(group = rep(c("Healthy", "Disease"), each = 30))  res3 <- run_foldchange(x = xsc, metadata = meta_sc, group = "group") print(res3$fc_table) #>     feature Disease_vs_Healthy #> 1  LPC 18:2             1.0099 #> 2 PA O-28:1             1.0005

text: Normalization Methods: sum: Divides sample total sum. Assumes similar total metabolite abundance across samples. median: Divides sample median value. robust outliers sum. specific_factor: Uses externally measured factors (e.g., osmolality urine, protein concentration plasma) metadata column. QC samples normalized separately using mean/median biological sample factors. pqn_global: Probabilistic Quotient Normalization using global median spectrum reference. Robust method accounts dilution effects. pqn_reference: PQN using specific reference sample. Useful one sample represents "ideal" composition. pqn_group: PQN using pooled QC samples reference. Recommended metabolomics data regular QC injections. quantile: Forces samples identical distributions. strong normalization may remove biological variation - use caution. col_rel_abundance: Computes relative abundance per feature (column). value divided sum column, feature's values sum 1 across samples. Useful comparing contribution feature across samples. Note normalization factors returned column sums. row_rel_abundance: Computes relative abundance per sample (row). value divided sum row, sample's values sum 1 across features. Equivalent "sum" normalization expressed proportions. Useful compositional data analysis. Note normalization factors returned row sums. Custom Normalization Factors: can provide numeric vector method custom normalization factors. vector length must equal number biological samples (excluding QCs). QC samples normalized using mean median factors.

text: Sample Exclusion: exclude parameter allows removal specific sample groups PCA: Common use: Exclude QC samples (exclude = c("QC", "SQC", "EQC")) Multiple groups can excluded: exclude = c("QC", "Blank", "Failed") Exclusion performed preprocessing steps Excluded samples included transformation/scaling calculations Input Data Requirements: function accepts data two forms: Pre-scaled data run_scale(): PCA performed directly without additional preprocessing. recommended workflow. Raw data: scale_method specified, function first apply optional transformation (via transform_method), scaling (via scale_method), performing PCA. PCA Methodology: PCA performed using singular value decomposition (SVD) via stats::prcomp(). function assumes data appropriately preprocessed (normalized, transformed, scaled) analysis. PCA performed without additional centering scaling (center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE) applied scaling step. Interpretation: PC Scores: Coordinates samples principal component space. Used visualization identifying patterns/clusters. PC Loadings: Contribution feature principal component. Features high absolute loadings drive separation along PC. Variance Explained: Percentage total variance captured PC. First PCs typically capture variance metabolomics data. Eigenvalues: Variance captured PC original units. Eigenvalue > 1 (Kaiser criterion) often used determine significant PCs. Scaling Recommendations: Auto-scaling ("auto"): Recommended PCA. Gives equal weight features regardless magnitude. Pareto-scaling ("pareto"): Alternative partially preserves variance structure. Can useful feature magnitudes carry biological meaning. Mean-centering ("mean"): centers data. Use features already comparable scales.

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data set.seed(123) x <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 50, mean = 100, sd = 20),             nrow = 100, ncol = 50) colnames(x) <- paste0("Feature", 1:50) rownames(x) <- paste0("Sample", 1:100)  metadata <- data.frame(   Sample = paste0("Sample", 1:100),   Group = rep(c("Control", "Treatment", "QC"), c(40, 40, 20)),   Batch = rep(1:4, each = 25),   Time = rep(c(0, 6, 12, 24), 25) )  # PCA on pre-scaled data (recommended) scaled_result <- run_scale(x, method = "auto") pca_result <- run_pca(scaled_result$data, metadata)  # PCA excluding QC samples pca_result2 <- run_pca(scaled_result$data, metadata,                         group = "Group",                        exclude = "QC")  # PCA with automatic scaling, excluding multiple groups pca_result3 <- run_pca(x, metadata,                         scale_method = "auto",                        group = "Group",                        exclude = c("QC", "Blank"))  # PCA with transformation and scaling pca_result4 <- run_pca(x, metadata,                         transform_method = "log2",                        scale_method = "pareto",                        group = "Group",                        exclude = "QC")  # View results print(pca_result) summary(pca_result) } # }

title: Reduce Multiple Data Frames by Common or Unique Column/Row Names — run_reduce
code: ## --- Example data --------------------------------------------------- df1 <- data.frame(a = 1:3, b = 4:6,  c = 7:9) df2 <- data.frame(a = 10:12, b = 13:15, d = 16:18) df3 <- data.frame(a = 19:21, b = 22:24, e = 25:27)  ## 1. Return columns common to ALL data frames (default) result_common <- run_reduce(df1, df2, df3) result_common$data          # columns: a, b #>    a  b #> 1  1  4 #> 2  2  5 #> 3  3  6 #> 4 10 13 #> 5 11 14 #> 6 12 15 #> 7 19 22 #> 8 20 23 #> 9 21 24 result_common$summary       # columns dropped per frame #> $df1 #> [1] "c" #>  #> $df2 #> [1] "d" #>  #> $df3 #> [1] "e" #>   ## 2. Return columns from ANY data frame (union, fill with NA) result_union <- run_reduce(df1, df2, df3, return_common = FALSE) result_union$data           # columns: a, b, c, d, e  (NAs where absent) #>    a  b  c  d  e #> 1  1  4  7 NA NA #> 2  2  5  8 NA NA #> 3  3  6  9 NA NA #> 4 10 13 NA 16 NA #> 5 11 14 NA 17 NA #> 6 12 15 NA 18 NA #> 7 19 22 NA NA 25 #> 8 20 23 NA NA 26 #> 9 21 24 NA NA 27  ## 3. Row-wise reduction ------------------------------------------- m1 <- data.frame(x = c(1, 2, 3), row.names = c("r1", "r2", "r3")) m2 <- data.frame(y = c(4, 5, 6), row.names = c("r1", "r3", "r4"))  result_rows <- run_reduce(m1, m2, check_cols = FALSE) result_rows$data            # rows: r1, r3 (common to both) #>    x y #> r1 1 4 #> r3 3 5  result_rows_union <- run_reduce(m1, m2, check_cols = FALSE,                                 return_common = FALSE) result_rows_union$data      # rows: r1, r2, r3, r4 (NAs where absent) #>     x  y #> r1  1  4 #> r2  2 NA #> r3  3  5 #> r4 NA  6  ## 4. Single data frame (warning, returned as-is) ------------------ result_single <- run_reduce(df1) #> Warning: Only one data frame was supplied. Returning it unchanged (no reduction performed).

title: Scale Metabolomics Data — run_scale
text: Scaling Methods: mean: Mean-centering . feature centered mean = 0 retains original variance. Useful features already similar scales want preserve relative variance information. auto: Auto-scaling (also called standardization unit variance scaling). feature mean-centered divided standard deviation, resulting mean = 0 SD = 1 features. gives equal weight features regardless original variance. Recommended : Principal Component Analysis (PCA) Hierarchical clustering Correlation analysis t-tests ANOVA univariate multivariate methods Auto-scaling can amplify noise low-variance features. pareto: Pareto-scaling. feature mean-centered divided square root standard deviation. provides compromise scaling auto-scaling, reducing influence large values preserving original variance structure. Recommended : PLS-DA (Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis) OPLS-DA (Orthogonal PLS-DA) sPLS-DA (Sparse PLS-DA) PLS-type methods Pareto-scaling generally preferred discriminant analysis balances importance large small features better auto-scaling. Choosing Scaling Method: choice depends downstream analysis: exploratory data analysis (PCA, clustering): auto-scaling classification/discrimination (PLS-DA): Pareto-scaling feature magnitudes carry meaning: mean-centering

text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Performs Descriptive Statistics on a Dataframe — run_summarytable
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Load necessary library library(gtsummary) library(dplyr) library(magrittr)  # Create a sample dataframe set.seed(123) sample_df <- data.frame(   Age = sample(c(18:65, NA), 100,                replace = TRUE, prob = c(rep(1, 48), 5)),   Gender = sample(c("Male", "Female", NA), 100,                   replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.48, 0.48, 0.04)),   Education = sample(c("Elementary", "High School", "Bachelors", "Masters", NA), 100,                      replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.40, 0.30, 0.15, 0.10, 0.05)),   Income = sample(c(seq(5000, 40000, by = 5000), NA), 100,                   replace = TRUE, prob = c(rep(1, 8), 2)),   Smoker = sample(c(1, 0, NA), 100,                   replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.25, 0.65, 0.10)),   Region = sample(c("North", "South", "East", "West", NA), 100,                   replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.24, 0.24, 0.24, 0.24, .04)) )  # Basic descriptive statistics # CORRECT: Piped data goes to 'df' argument automatically sample_df %>% run_summarytable()  # INCORRECT: Causes an error because 'sample_df' is passed to 'summarize_what' # sample_df %>% run_summarytable(sample_df)  # Descriptive statistics split by Gender, with custom header and labels sample_df %>%   run_summarytable(     split_by = "Gender",     split_by_header = "Participant Gender",     rename_variables = list(Age ~ "Age (Years)",                              Income ~ "Annual Income",                              Smoker ~ "Smoking Status")   )  # Descriptive statistics with median and IQR for continuous, and count only for categorical sample_df %>%   run_summarytable(     continuous_statistics = "medianIQR",     categorical_statistics = "n",     n_digits_continuous = c(0, 0),     n_digits_categorical = c(0, 0)   )  # Force 'Smoker' to be continuous (though conceptually it's not, for demonstration) # And display missing always (with custom text), with bold labels and italicized levels sample_df %>%   run_summarytable(     force_continuous = "Smoker",     display_missing = "always",     missing_text = "Data Unavailable",     missing_stat = "n",     bold_labels = TRUE,     italicize_levels = TRUE   )  # Sort categorical variables by frequency and calculate percentages by row # (not ideal, but for demonstration only) sample_df %>%   run_summarytable(     sort_categorical_variables_by = "frequency",     calc_percent_by = "row"   )  # Perform common comparative statistical tests sample_df %>%   run_summarytable(     split_by = "Gender",     add_inferential_pvalues = TRUE   ) } # }

title: Transform Metabolomics Data — run_transform
text: Transformation Methods: log2 / log10: Logarithmic transformations compress large values expand small values. Useful data spanning multiple orders magnitude. data contains zeros negative values, shift applied first. sqrt: Square root transformation. Moderate variance stabilization. Works zero values requires non-negative data. cbrt: Cube root transformation. Similar sqrt can handle negative values. Provides gentler compression log. vsn: Variance Stabilizing Normalization. Model-based method microarray analysis calibrates variance across intensity range. Requires 'vsn' package. Can computationally intensive; use num_cores parallel processing. glog: Generalized logarithm transformation. Hybrid log transformation handles low values better standard log. Requires 'pmp' package metadata QC sample labels. Use Method: log2/log10: common metabolomics. Use variance increases mean (heteroscedasticity). sqrt: variance proportional mean. vsn: need sophisticated variance modeling. Recommended large datasets clear intensity-dependent variance. glog: Alternative log many low-intensity values. Good LC-MS data detection limits.
